narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, an LSAT score of 33, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.70—metrics that stand out when compared to typical students, whose averages are higher at around 5.5-5.7 for those deciles, 36.7 for LSAT, and 3.21 for GPA. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing at 42.4%, a risk level that tipped the decision against your application. This prediction led to the rejection because the model prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar on their first try, viewing higher failure odds as a mismatch for their program. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom tier, which strongly signaled to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead, unlike the mid-range performance seen in most successful bar takers. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 33 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing by showing solid reasoning skills relative to expectations, even if below the typical 36.7 average. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 also helped somewhat, nudging things positively as a decent but not standout foundation compared to peers. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students and didn't sway things much, these top three elements—dominated by that low Year 3 decile—ultimately outweighed the positives. Many applicants in similar demographic situations overcome lower early rankings through targeted improvement, so consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 36 or above, or strengthening your academic record in any post-undergrad courses. Building a narrative around resilience, perhaps highlighting community leadership or bar prep commitment in a reapplication, could also address these gaps. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore prep programs tailored for students like you to demonstrate growth. With focused effort, you can reshape how models like this see your potential next time.
